question_title: What discount percentage will Sony offer on the 12-month PlayStation Plus Premium subscription (US) during Days of Play 2026?
summary: Days of Play 2026 is leaked to begin May 27 (announcement expected May 26), well within the resolution window. Historical PS Plus Premium 12-month discounts were 30% (2024) and 33% (2025). The billbil-kun leak notably omits PS Plus subscription discount details, creating uncertainty about whether discounts will be offered and at what level. However, PS Plus discounts have been included in every Days of Play historically, and Sony's strategy of keeping annual prices unchanged while raising short-term prices supports continuing to incentivize annual plan commitments. "Exactly 33%" is the modal outcome due to the strong 2025 precedent and corporate inertia in promotional percentages, but meaningful probability is spread across "30% to less than 33%" (possible reversion to 2024 levels) and "Greater than 33%" (possible aggressive annual plan push). The "no discount" outcome receives ~9% reflecting the unusual absence from the leak and Sony's aggressive pricing posture.

factors.description: Days of Play has included PS Plus discounts every year; extremely strong historical precedent for discount existing
2025 discount was 33% on Premium 12-month; Sony tends to maintain headline discount percentages year-over-year (30% in 2024 to 33% in 2025)
Sony kept annual PS Plus prices unchanged while raising short-term prices — strategic push for annual plan commitments supports maintaining generous annual discount
billbil-kun leak does NOT mention PS Plus subscription discounts; Notebookcheck says 'no indications of reduced rates on PS Plus plans' — could signal PS Plus discounts absent or reduced
factors.supports: No 12-month PS Plus Premium discount offered during Days of Play 2026 by May 31, 2026

factors.description: Sony's overall aggressive pricing strategy in 2026 (console price hikes, subscription price hikes, no PS5 console discounts at Days of Play) suggests less willingness to offer deep discounts
Sale starts May 27 with announcement May 26; by May 31 deadline, 4+ days of sale will have elapsed — high probability any PS Plus discounts would be visible by resolution
Dealabs characterizes 'usually up to 30%' as the historical PS Plus discount baseline — 33% was only used once (2025), 30% may be the more stable baseline

reasoning:
## Forecast Summary

**Question:** What discount percentage will Sony offer on the 12-month PlayStation Plus Premium subscription (US) during Days of Play 2026?

### Probability Distribution

| Option | Probability |
|--------|-------------|
| Less than 25% | 2% |
| 25% to less than 30% | 6% |
| 30% to less than 33% | 22% |
| **Exactly 33%** | **48%** |
| Greater than 33% | 13% |
| No discount by May 31 | 9% |

### Key Reasoning

- **"Exactly 33%" is the modal outcome** (48%): The 2025 precedent of 33% is the strongest anchor. Companies rarely change headline promotional percentages without a strategic reason. Sony's push to incentivize annual commitments (raising short-term prices while keeping annual unchanged) supports maintaining this level.

- **"30% to less than 33%" is second** (22%): A reversion to the 2024 level is plausible given Sony's aggressive pricing posture and the Dealabs characterization of "usually up to 30%" as the baseline.

- **"Greater than 33%"** (13%): If Sony is particularly aggressive about annual plan growth (ahead of GTA 6 in November 2026), a deeper discount is possible.

- **"No discount"** (9%): The notable absence of PS Plus details from billbil-kun's otherwise comprehensive leak creates genuine uncertainty. However, the 100% historical base rate of PS Plus inclusion in Days of Play keeps this low.

- **Confidence is moderate** (0.35 within 10pp): This is a 6-option MC question where the official announcement hasn't happened yet. The announcement (expected May 26) would immediately resolve most uncertainty.

resolution_criteria:
This question resolves based on the headline/maximum advertised percentage discount on the 12-month PlayStation Plus Premium subscription (new subscriber sign-up, US PlayStation Store) during Days of Play 2026, as announced on the official PlayStation Blog (https://blog.playstation.com/) or shown on the PlayStation Store / Days of Play hub (https://www.playstation.com/en-us/deals/days-of-play/) at any point between May 17, 2026 and May 31, 2026 (23:59 PT).

The percentage will be taken from Sony's official advertising language (e.g., "save 33% on a 12-month PlayStation Plus Premium membership"). If Sony advertises a range ("up to X%"), the upper bound X applies to the Premium 12-month tier specifically. If no explicit percentage is advertised but a discounted price is shown, the percentage will be computed as 1 − (discounted price ÷ standard $159.99 US price), and the bucket containing that computed value applies.

Options correspond to the discount percentage D:
- "Less than 25%": D < 25%
- "25% to less than 30%": 25% ≤ D < 30%
- "30% to less than 33%": 30% ≤ D < 33%
- "Exactly 33%": D = 33% (rounded to nearest whole percent in Sony's advertising)
- "Greater than 33%": D > 33%
- "No 12-month PS Plus Premium discount offered during Days of Play 2026 by May 31, 2026"
fine_print:
If Days of Play 2026 has not begun by May 31, 2026, 23:59 PT, or if the promotion is announced but does not include a 12-month PS Plus Premium subscription discount by that time, the question resolves "No 12-month PS Plus Premium discount offered during Days of Play 2026 by May 31, 2026."

If Sony renames or restructures the Premium tier, the equivalent top-tier 12-month PlayStation Plus subscription (the one including the classics catalog / cloud streaming, previously known as Premium or Deluxe) will be used.

If the PlayStation Blog announcement and PlayStation Store advertised percentages differ, the PlayStation Blog headline language for the US region takes precedence. If neither source is accessible, [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) reporting the official Sony-advertised Days of Play 2026 Premium discount will be used.

input.anchor: Historical base rate: Days of Play has included PS Plus discounts every year since the event began. In the two most recent years, the Premium 12-month discount was 30% (2024) and 33% (2025). Prior anchor for discount existing: ~92%. Most likely level conditional on existence: 33% (matching 2025).
input.assessment:
The strongest signal is the historical precedent: Days of Play has consistently included PS Plus discounts, and the trend from 2024 (30%) to 2025 (33%) is upward. Sony's strategy of keeping annual prices unchanged while raising short-term prices aligns with offering attractive annual plan discounts to drive long-term subscriber lock-in.

The main counterweight is that the billbil-kun leak — usually comprehensive and reliable — does NOT mention PS Plus subscription discounts. Multiple outlets note this absence. This creates genuine uncertainty about whether PS Plus discounts will be included this year, or whether the discount level might change.

However, PS Plus discounts are digital deals that may be announced separately from the physical retail deals that billbil-kun typically leaks. The official announcement (expected May 26) would be the natural venue for PS Plus discount details.

The 'Exactly 33%' outcome is my modal estimate because: (1) Sony used this level in 2025, (2) companies tend to maintain headline discount percentages for consistency, (3) it aligns with Sony's annual plan push strategy. '30% to less than 33%' is my second choice, representing a possible pullback to the 2024 level. 'Greater than 33%' is plausible if Sony is particularly aggressive about annual subscriptions ahead of GTA 6.

The 'no discount' option at 9% reflects the small but real possibility that Sony departs from precedent, especially given the absence of PS Plus details from the leak. But the base rate of PS Plus being included in Days of Play is essentially 100% historically.
input.calibration_notes: The 'Exactly 33%' at 48% may seem high for a specific bucket, but 33% is the natural Schelling point given the 2025 precedent. Companies rarely change headline promotional percentages year-over-year without reason. The main risk to this estimate is on the downside (reversion to 30%) or the 'no discount' scenario. My total probability mass on 30%+ (including 33% and above) is 83%, which seems well-calibrated given the strong historical precedent.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Whether PS Plus discounts are absent from the leak because they don't exist, or because they're announced separately. 2. Whether Sony's aggressive pricing posture in 2026 extends to reducing/eliminating Days of Play PS Plus discounts. 3. Whether the discount level stays at 33% or reverts to 30%.
input.tool_audit: Research sub-agent provided comprehensive findings including leaked dates, leaked deals, and historical context. Web search and fetch_url were effective at extracting details from multiple sources. The Dealabs source was particularly valuable for confirming what was/wasn't in the leak. No tool failures.
input.update_triggers: 1. Official Sony Days of Play 2026 announcement (expected May 26) — would immediately resolve most uncertainty. 2. Additional billbil-kun leak specifically about PS Plus — would clarify the discount level. 3. Any news about PS Plus promotional strategy changes at Sony.
input.process_reflection: The research pipeline worked well for this question. The research() sub-agent gathered extensive background, and direct web searches helped fill in specific details. The main challenge was interpreting the 'absence of evidence' in the leak — whether the lack of PS Plus discount details is informative about Sony's plans or just a limitation of the leak's scope. The multiple source cross-referencing was essential for establishing this distinction.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

**Factors checked against trace:** All 7 factors are grounded in the research trace.

1. **Factor 1 (historical precedent):** Confirmed by multiple sources in the trace including Everyeye.it, PlayStation Norway page, and worldview research entries showing consistent Days of Play PS Plus discounts 2022-2025.

2. **Factor 2 (2025=33%, 2024=30%):** Directly confirmed by the Norway PlayStation page ("Save up to 33% on select 12-month PlayStation Plus plans") and Everyeye.it ("30% off Premium annual in 2024, 33% off Premium annual in 2025").

3. **Factor 3 (annual prices unchanged, short-term raised):** Confirmed by The Verge article (monthly $10.99, quarterly $27.99 increases) and research report noting "Annual plan prices were not increased."

4. **Factor 4 (leak omits PS Plus):** Confirmed by Notebookcheck fetch ("there are no indications of reduced rates on PlayStation Plus plans") and Dealabs fetch (PS Plus discounts described only as historical "habituellement" context, not 2026-specific).

5. **Factor 5 (aggressive pricing):** Confirmed by multiple sources on console price hikes (April 2026), subscription hikes (May 2026), and no PS5 console discounts expected.

6. **Factor 6 (May 27 start):** Confirmed by billbil-kun leak via multiple outlets (Insider Gaming, Push Square, Notebookcheck, etc.).

7. **Factor 7 (Dealabs "usually 30%"):** Confirmed by Dealabs fetch showing "habituellement" (usually/typically) up to 30% as historical baseline.

**Double-counting:** Factors 1 and 2 draw on overlapping historical data but address distinct questions (existence vs. level). Mild overlap, not impactful.

**Wrong-direction factors:** None found. All logit signs match their described effects.

**Resolution criteria:** Properly retrieved and engaged with (specific sources, $159.99 base price, May 17-31 window, PlayStation Blog precedence).

**Contradictory assessment:** None. The assessment aligns with the factor weights — modal estimate on "Exactly 33%" with meaningful spread to alternatives.

**Worldview consistency:** One older worldview entry (slug: 2279eb37) claims 25% as typical, but it was generated without web tools and based on stale training data. The agent's trace research with live web tools found the correct 30%/33% figures. The agent used the better-sourced data — no contradiction that would change the forecast.

**Counterargument quality:** Substantive — identifies leak silence as informative, challenges the 33% recency anchor, notes Sony's aggressive pricing posture, and considers underweighting of "Greater than 33%." Genuine adversarial reasoning.

**Self-confidence:** 0.35 is well-calibrated for a 6-option multiple-choice question with genuine uncertainty about both existence and level of the discount.

**Probability assessment:** The distribution (48% Exactly 33%, 22% 30-33%, 13% >33%, 9% No discount, 6% 25-30%, 2% <25%) is reasonable given the evidence. My independent estimate would be broadly similar (~50/20/10/7/8/5), with no meaningful divergence.

**Tool audit:** Agent claims "No tool failures" — metrics confirm 0 errors across 24 calls. Consistent.

No errors found.
